perturbation.original_text: The receiving party agrees that it will at all times maintain the confidentiality of any Confidential Information communicated to it by or on behalf of the disclosing party; provided that in the event the receiving party becomes legally compelled (by deposition, interrogatory, requests for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Page 1 ​ of 4 Information, or the receiving party determines that it is obligated by statute or governmental regulation to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the receiving party shall provide the disclosing party with prompt NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT written notice of such requirement so that the disclosing party, if possible, may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees that it will endeavor to maintain the confidentiality of any Confidential Information communicated to it by or on behalf of the disclosing party; provided that in the event the receiving party becomes legally compelled (by deposition, interrogatory, requests for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information, or the receiving party subjectively determines that it is obligated by statute or governmental regulation to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the receiving party shall provide the disclosing party with notice of such requirement when practical so that the disclosing party may seek a protective order or other remedy if they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality and provide notice. Changing 'at all times maintain' to 'endeavor to maintain' makes the confidentiality obligation less strict. Replacing 'determines that it is obligated' with 'subjectively determines that it is obligated' introduces a subjective standard. Removing 'prompt written' and replacing 'if possible' with 'if they see fit' weakens the notice requirement, creating ambiguity about when and how notice must be given and if a protective order should be sought.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) outlines the process for seeking a protective order to protect confidential information during discovery. By weakening the notice requirement and introducing a subjective standard for determining legal obligation, the modified text undermines the disclosing party's ability to effectively seek a protective order under FRCP 26(c). The 'when practical' and 'if they see fit' clauses contradict the duty to protect confidential information during legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that undermine the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement and the notification process, potentially conflicting with legal standards for protecting confidential information. The original text's 'at all times maintain' created a clear obligation, while 'endeavor to maintain' weakens this obligation. Similarly, changing 'determines that it is obligated' to 'subjectively determines' makes the obligation to disclose less objective and verifiable. The phrase 'when practical' further weakens the obligation to provide notice, making it unclear when the receiving party must notify the disclosing party. These changes make the contract's obligation ambiguous. While the provided snippets do not directly address FRCP 26(c) directly, the Law Explanation provided clearly states that these modifications undermine the disclosing party's ability to effectively seek a protective order under FRCP 26(c).

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information shall not be copied, reproduced in any form or stored in a retrieval system or data base by the receiving party without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, except for such copies and storage as may reasonably be required internally by the receiving party in connection with the Opportunity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information should not be unnecessarily copied, reproduced in any readily accessible form or stored in a publicly accessible retrieval system or database by the receiving party without the general consent of the disclosing party, except for such copies and storage as are subjectively believed to be required internally by the receiving party in connection with the Opportunity.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should not' weakens the prohibition on copying and storing confidential information. The insertion of 'unnecessarily', 'readily accessible form' and 'publicly accessible' adds ambiguity, allowing the receiving party to argue that certain copies or storage methods are permissible. Replacing 'prior written consent' with 'general consent' introduces vagueness about the form and timing of consent. The change from 'may reasonably be required' to 'subjectively believed to be required' makes the exception subjective and less objectively justifiable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation, which includes unauthorized copying or storage. By weakening the prohibition on copying and storage and introducing subjective exceptions, the modified text undermines the protections afforded by the DTSA. The alterations provide loopholes for the receiving party to argue that its actions did not constitute misappropriation, hindering the disclosing party's ability to enforce its trade secret rights under the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws. The original text uses 'shall not,' creating a clear prohibition, while the revised text uses 'should not,' which introduces ambiguity. The phrase 'unnecessarily copied' further weakens the prohibition, as it allows for subjective interpretation of what is necessary. Similarly, 'readily accessible form' and 'publicly accessible' introduce further ambiguity, making it harder to prove misappropriation. The change from 'prior written consent' to 'general consent' also weakens the requirement. Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, discusses civil actions for trade secret misappropriation. By introducing ambiguity and weakening prohibitions, the modified contract text makes it more difficult for the disclosing party to enforce its trade secret rights, potentially contradicting the intent of the law. Snippet 2 is a dead link and cannot be used to justify a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall not directly or indirectly contact any shareholders, officers, employees, affiliates, agents, lending institutions, customers or suppliers of the disclosing party in connection with a possible transaction involving the disclosing party without the prior written consent of the disclosing party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party should not directly or indirectly attempt to contact any shareholders, officers, key employees, affiliates, known agents, primary lending institutions, major customers, or critical suppliers of the disclosing party in connection with a probable transaction possibly involving the disclosing party without considering obtaining consent of the disclosing party, which consent may be withheld at their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Several changes introduce ambiguity and weaken the obligation. Replacing 'shall not' with 'should not' makes the restriction less mandatory. Qualifiers such as 'key', 'known', 'primary', 'major', and 'critical' narrow the scope of the restriction, leaving room for interpretation about who is covered. Changing 'possible transaction involving' to 'probable transaction possibly involving' makes the trigger for the restriction less clear. Replacing 'prior written consent' with 'considering obtaining consent' significantly weakens the consent requirement. Replacing 'shall not be unreasonably withheld' with 'may be withheld at their discretion' eliminates the standard of reasonableness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Unauthorized contact with shareholders, officers, or employees could lead to the dissemination of material non-public information, potentially influencing trading decisions. By weakening the restrictions on contacting these individuals and removing the reasonableness standard for withholding consent, the modified text increases the risk of insider trading and violates the spirit, if not the letter, of Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5.

More in this Section Notice Rule Notice of Filing and Immediate Effectiveness of Proposed Rule Change To Amend the Exchanges Pricing Schedule at Options 7, Section 3 National Securities Exchanges, Nasdaq MRX, LLC (MRX) Public Comments Submit a Comment on SR-MRX-2023-04 View Received Comments Notice Rule (34-97156) Title Notice of Withdrawal of Proposed Rule Change To Amend the Exchange Pricing Schedule at Options 7, Section 3 Release Number 34-97156 SEC Issue Date March 17, 2023 Federal Register Publish Date March 23, 2023 Document Citation 88 FR 17632 Resources Notice Rule: SEC Issued Version ( pdf 114.92 KB) Notice Rule: Federal Register Version Last Reviewed or Updated: Aug. 31, 2023 Resources Notice Rule: SEC Issued Version ( pdf 175.73 KB) Notice Rule: Federal Register Version Exhibit 5 Details Public Comments Due March 14, 2023 File Number SR-MRX-2023-04 Rule Type Notice Release Number 34-96924 SEC Issue Date Feb. 14, 2023 Federal Register Publish Date Feb. 21, 2023 Document Citation 88 FR 10585
perturbation.justification:
The original contract strictly prohibits contact with specified parties without prior written consent, ensuring compliance and preventing potential misuse of information. The changed text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall not' with 'should not,' introducing ambiguity. It further narrows the scope of the restriction with qualifiers like 'key employees,' 'known agents,' and 'major customers,' and weakens the consent requirement by changing 'prior written consent' to 'considering obtaining consent' and replacing 'shall not be unreasonably withheld' with 'may be withheld at their discretion.'

According to the LLM explanation, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Unauthorized contact with shareholders, officers, or employees could lead to the dissemination of material non-public information, potentially influencing trading decisions. Because the changes weaken restrictions on contacting these individuals and remove the reasonableness standard for withholding consent, the modified text increases the risk of insider trading. The original text provided a clear, enforceable rule, while the modified text introduces several ambiguities that could lead to legal disputes and undermine the original intent of preventing unauthorized contact. Because the original contract prevents actions that could violate insider trading regulations, and the changed contract makes those violations more likely, this is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the disclosing party and its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, representatives and professional advisors from any damage, loss, cost or liability (including reasonable legal fees and the cost of enforcing this Agreement) owed to a third party arising out of the breach by the receiving party of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party may indemnify, potentially defend, and possibly hold harmless the disclosing party and its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, representatives and professional advisors from certain damages, losses, costs or liabilities (excluding legal fees unless absolutely necessary and the cost of occasionally enforcing this Agreement) purportedly owed to a third party arguably arising out of a clear breach by the receiving party of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will indemnify, defend and hold harmless' to 'may indemnify, potentially defend, and possibly hold harmless' makes the indemnification obligation discretionary. Adding 'certain', 'excluding legal fees unless absolutely necessary', and 'occasionally enforcing this agreement' limits the scope of indemnification. Using 'purportedly owed' and 'arguably arising out of' introduces doubt about the validity of the third-party claim and the causal link to the breach. Adding 'clear' before breach raises the standard of proof needed to trigger the indemnification obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Contract Principles - Enforceability of Indemnification Clauses
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no specific statute being violated, this change undermines the very purpose of an indemnification clause, which is to provide clear protection against specified losses. Common law principles require contractual obligations to be clear and enforceable. By making the indemnification obligation discretionary and adding numerous qualifications, the modified text renders the clause significantly less enforceable, contradicting the intention to provide a clear and reliable remedy for breaches.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: indemnify | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you indemnify To indemnify, also known as indemnity or indemnification, means compensating a person for damages or losses they have incurred or will incur related to a specified accident, incident, or event. Typically, parties make a written agreement in which one party (indemnitor) promises to indemnify the other party (indemnitee) for future specified losses. The more common form of an indemnification agreement is the insurance policy. This often happens when an insurance company, as part of an individual's insurance policy, agrees to indemnify the insured person for losses that the insured person incurred due to accident or property damage. In this sort of agreement, party A would hire insurance company B. If party A and party C are involved in some incident that results in property or personal damage (ex: a car accident), insurance company B would assume any liability for which Party A might be responsible. After that, insurance company B would typically sue party C to recover damages. See Hercules Inc. et al v. United States, 516 U.S. 417 (1996). [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract contract law liability Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities insurance PROPERTY contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text creates a clear indemnification obligation, whereas the changed text makes indemnification discretionary and limits its scope, undermining the purpose of an indemnification clause. The scraped snippet defines 'indemnify' as 'compensating a person for damages or losses they have incurred or will incur related to a specified accident, incident, or event', suggesting a clear obligation, which the changed text weakens significantly. The modifications introduce ambiguity and reduce enforceability, thus contradicting the intent of providing a reliable remedy as described in the Law Explanation.
